_meta.notes: Halfling homeland. Built on a coral reef miles offshore — pontoon dwellings anchored to dead coral; living coral is sacred and cutting it is a capital offense. The freelance navy operates from here. The dawn-meal is the only meal of the day; foreigners are politely tolerated but openly judged for their snacking habits.

tagline: Pontoons over reef. Six ships, four ribbons, one meal a day.
ambientPool: The pontoons rise and fall on the reef-tide in a rhythm everyone here has internalized.
Smoke from a single cookfire rises only at dawn and is gone by mid-morning.
A Reef-cutter checks knots on a privateer vessel without looking down.
A Drowncaller eyes a distant sail and decides, silently, whether it is worth saving.
The reef itself is half-visible through the clear water — alive, slow-moving, dangerous to cut.

rooms.summary: Where the tidal stone causeway meets the Anchorage. Uncovered twice a day by tide; underwater the rest of the time. Foreigners arrive here at low tide or not at all.
ambientPool: The tide is going out; the causeway emerges section by section.
A halfling sentry on a low pontoon watches the causeway without moving.
A salt-keeper carries dried fish in a waxed sack across the causeway, unhurried.
Far behind, the metropolis glints. Far ahead, the open ocean.

exits.narrative: Onto the main pontoon decking, toward the navy yard.
Into the communal galley where dawn-meal is taken.

rooms.summary: The standing navy's home dock. Six small fast ships at anchor; the flagship Coral Anchor is the largest of them and would be considered small in any other navy.
ambientPool: A Tidehands officer reviews a chart with quiet concentration.
A reef-cutter privateer ties up alongside, accepts a brief inspection, and is sent on.
Two halfling crew share a private joke without smiling.
The Coral Anchor's four ribbons hang from its mast — quietly, expensively earned.

exits.narrative: Across the pontoons to the galley.
Out to the reef-edge platform — the seaward boundary.

rooms.summary: A long open-sided dining pontoon. Used only at dawn. The rest of the day it sits empty, the long tables gleaming with the previous morning's salt-cure.
ambientPool: The galley is empty at midday. The smell of breakfast hours-old still hangs.
A salt-keeper stocks the galley for tomorrow at noon — the work is unhurried, methodical.
Foreigners who come here at the wrong time find themselves alone with their own embarrassment.
A young halfling of seven, who will eat their first dawn-meal in three weeks, walks the empty hall imagining it.

rooms.summary: The seaward boundary of the Anchorage. A platform of dead coral columns jutting out over open water. Drowncallers stand watch here. Living coral is visible below — colors no other place sees.
ambientPool: A Drowncaller marks a small slate; they have logged a sail at the edge of sight.
Living coral pulses slowly in the water below — yellow, sun-orange, the deep red they call 'inside-of-the-mouth'.
A salt wind carries from the open ocean; the platform planks creak in answer.
A Tidehands captain watches the same horizon, decides nothing, returns to the yard.
